title: Streetline-style real-estate interior edit: straighten, HDR-look tone, white balance, window pull, outlet clean-up
grounding_note: VERBATIM from Kimberley's Upwork brief (db_i 3084): Dutch real-estate photographer needs an editing partner for a 25-30 photo batch; named services = "HDR merge / exposure blending", "Vertical and horizontal line correction", "Color correction and white balance adjustment", "Natural window views (window pull)", "Removal of distracting objects where necessary", "General image enhancement while maintaining a realistic appearance"; quality bar = "Natural-looking colors", "Bright but realistic interiors", "Straight walls and vertical lines", "Preserving outdoor views through windows", "No oversaturated colors or unrealistic HDR effects", style "similar to Streetline: bright, clean, realistic, and professional"; delivery = "High-resolution JPG files" with "Consistent editing style across all images". REASONABLY ENRICHED for a runnable single-image benchmark: the brief is a 25-30 image batch, so I instantiate the named edit recipe on ONE representative hero living-room frame — the same chain is the per-image recipe that would be looped across the set. HONEST CAPABILITY MAPPING required by the sheet: (a) true multi-bracket HDR MERGE is not a single connector tool, so the "HDR / exposure blending" requirement is delivered as the industry-equivalent single-frame tonal recovery (apply_auto_tone + selective highlight/shadow/exposure recovery) that produces the bright-but-realistic Streetline look without an unrealistic HDR effect — exactly what the client asks for ("no unrealistic HDR effects"); (b) "window pull" is implemented legally as select_by_prompt(windows) -> highlight/exposure pulldown on that mask (selective tonal edit, explicitly allowed), NOT a prompt-based composite; (c) "removal of distracting objects" via AI is [X] not available, so I scope it to the connector-legal case the brief allows ("where necessary"): a single small fixed-color distractor (a wall power outlet) is selected and solid-filled to match the wall via image_fill_area — no generative fill, no AI object removal. No Canva, no Express [W] widget track, no generative fill, no compositing.
one_line_ask: Edit a Dutch real-estate interior to a bright, clean, realistic Streetline-style finish — straighten verticals, recover an HDR-look exposure, fix white balance, pull the blown-out window view back, neutralize one distracting wall outlet, and deliver a high-res JPG.
full_brief:
Kimberley is launching a real-estate photography business in the Netherlands and needs a reliable editing partner for an initial batch of 25-30 listing photos shot for the Dutch/European market (Streetline-style expectations). Using ONE representative hero frame of a living room, run the standard per-image edit recipe end-to-end on the Adobe connector so it can be looped across the whole set with a consistent style:

1) Load the client's supplied hero frame into CC storage (the well-exposed hero interior).
2) Auto-straighten the hero to fix tilted verticals/horizontals (walls must read straight), then crop to clean bounds removing the rotation wedge.
3) Apply auto-tone, then recover exposure / lift the dark portions / tame the highlights to build the bright-but-realistic, HDR-style interior WITHOUT an oversaturated or unrealistic HDR look.
4) Neutralize the warm/cool color cast: correct color temperature for accurate white balance, then gently lift vibrance (no oversaturation) so colors stay natural.
5) Window pull: select the windows by prompt, pull their highlights and exposure down on that mask so the outdoor view (garden/street) reads through the glass instead of blowing out white.
6) Clean one distracting element where necessary: select the small white wall power outlet and solid-fill it to match the surrounding wall color (a legal solid-fill clean-up, not AI object removal).
7) Apply a final consistent, realistic look and retrieve the delivered high-resolution JPG master for client hand-off.

Deliverable: one high-resolution, Streetline-ready JPG of the interior — straight verticals, neutral white balance, bright realistic interior, intact through-window exterior view, distractor cleaned — that defines the repeatable editing style for the remaining images in the batch.

inputs.name: hero_livingroom_interior.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Photoreal real-estate listing photo of an empty modern Dutch living room shot on a wide-angle lens at chest height, taken slightly tilted so the vertical wall lines lean a few degrees (un-straightened, as a raw real-estate capture). Bright daylight interior but the tall garden-facing windows on the left are slightly blown out to near-white. Light oak engineered floor, white plaster walls, a grey three-seat sofa, a low wooden TV console, a green potted plant. One small white double power outlet visible low on the right-hand wall as a distracting element. Realistic mixed daylight/tungsten color cast (slightly warm), natural shadows, real-world imperfections, fine sensor noise, documentary architectural-photography feel, NOT a glossy CGI render. 3:2, high resolution.
inputs.realism_notes: Must look like an unedited raw real-estate capture: intentionally tilted verticals, a real (not stylized) slight warm white-balance cast, and genuinely near-blown windows so the straighten/white-balance/window-pull steps have something real to correct. Visible small wall outlet so the fill_area clean-up has a real fixed-color target. Real floor grain and sensor noise, no CGI gloss.

outputs.name: livingroom_streetline_final.jpg
outputs.spec: High-resolution JPG (3:2, full sensor resolution preserved, sRGB), Streetline-style finish: straightened verticals, cropped clean (no rotation wedge), bright-but-realistic interior with recovered highlights/shadows, neutral white balance, natural non-oversaturated color, windows pulled so the exterior garden view reads through the glass, single wall outlet neutralized to wall color, consistent realistic final look. Delivered as the client-approved master that sets the editing style for the remaining 24-29 images.

connector_workflow.note: Begin upload of the client's hero living-room frame into CC storage; returns upload session + target href.
Finalize the hero upload to a usable CC asset URN/presigned URL — the base image the whole edit chain consumes.
Auto-straighten the tilted hero so wall verticals/horizontals read straight — the brief's 'Vertical and horizontal line correction'.
Crop away the rotation wedge introduced by straightening for a clean rectangular frame.
Baseline auto-tone to set sane exposure/contrast before targeted HDR-look recovery.
Lift the dark/shadow portions to open up the interior — the 'bright but realistic' Streetline look, exposure-blend equivalent on a single frame.
Pull global highlights down to recover blown areas without an unrealistic HDR halo (brief: 'no unrealistic HDR effects').
Correct the warm/cool cast for accurate white balance — the brief's 'Color correction and white balance adjustment'.
Gently lift vibrance only (no saturation push) so colors stay natural — brief: 'Natural-looking colors', 'No oversaturated colors'.
Select the windows by prompt to build the mask for the window pull.
On the window mask, pull highlights down so the blown glass recovers toward the exterior garden view — selective masked tonal edit.
Further reduce exposure on the same window mask to fully resolve the through-glass garden view — completes 'Natural window views (window pull)'.
Select the small white wall power outlet — the single distracting element to clean 'where necessary'.
Solid-fill the outlet selection with the wall's neutral color (legal solid-color fill, NOT AI object removal) to neutralize the distractor.
Apply one consistent realistic interior look (a neutral bright-clean preset, not a stylized filter) to lock the repeatable per-image style across the batch.
Retrieve a presigned URL for the edited result in CC storage to surface the delivered high-resolution Streetline-ready master JPG for client hand-off.

chaining_note: Strict single-thread output->input chain. Input loads first: hero_livingroom_interior.jpg -> (1 init -> 2 finalize) hero_asset. The edit chain then flows hero_asset -> 3 auto_straighten -> straightened_img -> 4 crop_to_bounds -> cropped_img -> 5 auto_tone -> autotoned_img -> 6 dark_portions -> shadows_lifted_img -> 7 highlights -> highlights_tamed_img -> 8 color_temperature -> wb_corrected_img -> 9 vibrance_and_saturation -> color_balanced_img. The window pull forks a mask off color_balanced_img: 10 select_by_prompt -> window_mask, which together with color_balanced_img feeds 11 highlights(masked) -> window_highlights_pulled_img -> 12 exposure(masked, same window_mask) -> window_pulled_img. The outlet clean-up forks again: 13 select_by_prompt(window_pulled_img) -> outlet_mask, then 14 fill_area(window_pulled_img + outlet_mask) -> outlet_cleaned_img -> 15 apply_preset -> final_look_img -> 16 get_presigned_urls -> livingroom_streetline_final.jpg. Every step after the initial asset load consumes a prior step's named output; the deliverable JPG is the terminal product of the chain.
difficulty_rationale: Long-horizon and wide-mix: 16 connector calls, all server="adobe", 14 distinct Adobe tools spanning three groups (asset upload/delivery, geometry, tonal/color, masked selection, solid-fill clean-up, preset). Difficulty comes from honest capability mapping under hard constraints: the brief's tempting-but-illegal asks (true HDR merge, AI distractor removal, prompt-based window replacement) are each re-expressed as connector-legal equivalents — single-frame tonal recovery for the HDR look, masked highlight/exposure pulldown for the window pull, and solid-fill for the one distractor — with no generative fill, no compositing, no upscaling, and no Express/Canva. The masked two-stage window pull (select -> highlights -> exposure on the same mask) and the select->fill outlet clean-up require correct mask reuse and order. All steps are [C] headless-confirmed, so the whole chain is autonomously runnable while still reading as a real Streetline-grade per-image editing recipe.

reverify.banned_capability_check: No banned/[X] capability is invoked anywhere in the chain. HDR is delivered as single-frame tonal recovery (auto_tone + dark_portions/highlights/exposure), the window pull is a masked selective tonal edit (select_by_prompt -> masked image_adjust_highlights/image_adjust_exposure), and the one distractor is a SOLID-COLOR image_fill_area of a selected region (not AI object removal, not generative fill, not compositing). No image_crop_and_resize/reframe-crop, no upscale/super-resolution, no text-to-image, no from-scratch art/logo, no bg-replace-by-prompt, no Canva/Express widget track. image_fill_area is used only as a solid fill within the existing canvas (no canvas growth). Every step is a real Adobe connector tool in exec_mode C (headless). Step 16 uses asset_get_presigned_urls (real connector tool) to surface the edit-chain output for delivery instead of the imprecise asset_finalize_file_upload. All-real-connector, all gen-free.
reverify.notes: Structural defects resolved. (1) The delivery step uses asset_get_presigned_urls to retrieve a presigned URL for the edit output rather than finalizing a non-existent upload session. (2) The previously dead-weight bracket_asset (window_bracket_reference.jpg, loaded via two upload calls but never consumed by any inputs_from) has been removed entirely — the input, both upload steps, and every narrative reference to it — because the honest single-frame mapping recovers the window view via a masked tonal edit on the hero frame itself (whose windows are near-blown), and feeding a second exposure into a tonal adjustment would constitute the banned HDR/exposure-blend composite the spec avoids. Steps renumbered 1..16; tool_call_count is now 16; distinct_adobe_tools remains 14 (asset_initialize_file_upload and asset_finalize_file_upload load the hero at steps 1-2; asset_get_presigned_urls delivers the master at step 16). The redundant inline-preview pseudo-output (livingroom_streetline_final_preview) and its asset_inline_preview step remain cut. Task remains real, coherent, and fully connector-doable.
